html:
[215]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 須臾 - A short period of time, often used to indicate a brief moment.
  • 珍肴旨酒 - A combination of delicious dishes and fine wine.
  • 羅列滿案 - Describes the arrangement of food and drink on a table.
  • 悶酒寡歡 - Drinking alone and feeling depressed.
  • 今夕幸逢嘉客 - Tonight we are fortunate to have a good guest.
  • 盍行一風雅令 - Why don't we play a game of refined poetry?
  • 領頭人不俗 - The host is not ordinary.
  • 開口便道得個風雅 - The moment he speaks, he mentions something elegant.
  • 豈敢攀風雅 - How dare I claim to be elegant?
  • 隨舉四書一句 - Just take a phrase from the Four Books.
  • 下接古人名 - Follow it with an ancient person's name.
  • 合者免飲 - If it matches, they don't have to drink.
  • 否則罰依金谷 - Otherwise, they must drink according to the rules of the Jin Valley.
  • 引大觥先酌某 - The host takes a large cup of wine to toast someone.
  • 賓不奪主為辭 - Guests should not take away the host's role.
  • 覆掌而起 - Turn over the palm and stand up.
  • 魏征 - Wei Zheng, a famous minister during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 武子瘦詞 - A term referring to a type of poetry that is concise and elegant.
  • 漢儒射策 - A term referring to a type of poetry competition during the Han Dynasty.
  • 後來屈上 - Later, they yield to the superior.
  • 大巫壓小巫矣 - The great sorcerer suppresses the small sorcerer.
  • 五谷不生 - Crops do not grow.
  • 田光 - Tian Guang, a historical figure.
  • 載戢干戈 - Put down weapons.
  • 畢戰 - Bi Zhan, a historical figure.
  • 二姊工力悉敵 - The second sister's skill is equal to the first.
  • 詞壇角兩雌 - Two queens on the poetry stage.
  • 白眼視 - Look at with a white eye (disdain).
  • 剔發澤戲彈其面 - Playfully tickle her face with a feather.
  • 坐於塗炭 - Sit in mud and charcoal.
  • 黑臀 - Hei Dun, a historical figure.
  • 扭腹三四 - Twist the abdomen a few times.
  • 妮子此中真有左癖 - The girl really has a left-handed bias.
  • 寡、寡……寡…… - Stuttering.
  • 我輩誰個不寡 - Who among us is not alone?
  • 要汝道得許多字 - You need to say many words.
  • 引杯欲罰 - Take the cup to punish.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 四書 - The Four Books, a collection of texts central to Confucianism.
  • 金谷 - Refers to the Jin Valley, a place associated with a famous poetry competition.
  • 孟子 - Mencius, a prominent Confucian philosopher.
  • 魏征 - Wei Zheng, a famous minister during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 武子 - Refers to a type of poetry that is concise and elegant.
  • 漢儒射策 - A type of poetry competition during the Han Dynasty.
  • 田光 - Tian Guang, a historical figure.
  • 畢戰 - Bi Zhan, a historical figure.
  • 黑臀 - Hei Dun, a historical figure.

Proper Nouns

  • 四書 - The Four Books.
  • 金谷 - The Jin Valley.
  • 孟子 - Mencius.
  • 魏征 - Wei Zheng.
  • 武子 - A type of poetry.
  • 漢儒射策 - A type of poetry competition.
  • 田光 - Tian Guang.
  • 畢戰 - Bi Zhan.
  • 黑臀 - Hei Dun.

[216]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鳳兮鳳兮 - Classical exclamation invoking the phoenix, symbolizing nobility and virtue.
  • 故是一鳳，何礙？ - Translates to 'it is still a phoenix, what harm?' Likely conveys that being a phoenix (a symbol of virtue) is not a hindrance.
  • 格格而吐 - '格格' is an onomatopoeic term meaning 'giggling' or 'laughing softly'; '而吐' means 'and said' or 'and spoke'.
  • 寡人好勇 - '寡人' is a humble self-reference used by ancient rulers, meaning 'I, the humble one'; '好勇' means 'enjoys bravery' or 'is fond of courage'.
  • 其直如矢 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'as straight as an arrow,' often used to describe someone who is honest or straightforward.
  • 正色而言 - '正色' means 'with a serious or stern expression'; '而言' means 'and said'.
  • 泰伯其可谓至德也已矣 - '泰伯' refers to Taibo, one of the Three Descendants of the Duke of Zhou, known for his virtue; '其可谓至德也已矣' means 'can truly be called the highest virtue'.
  • 朋友之交也 - '朋友之交' refers to the relationship between friends, emphasizing loyalty and trust.
  • 妹年幼，勉为众姊续貂 - '续貂' is a metaphor from the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian), referring to adding a dog's tail to a fox's fur, symbolizing something inferior added to something superior. Here, it likely means 'to save face' or 'to make up for a lack'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鳳 - The phoenix is a mythical bird in Chinese culture, symbolizing virtue, nobility, and the ideal ruler.
  • 王猛 - Wang Mang was a usurper who established the short-lived Xin Dynasty, often associated with tyranny and ambition.
  • 陽貨 - Yang Hu was a minister during the Spring and Autumn period, known for his cunning and ruthlessness.
  • 豫讓 - Yu Rang was a loyal assassin who served the state of Zhao, famous for his unwavering devotion to his lord.
  • 第五倫 - Dì Wǔ Lún was a high-ranking official during the Eastern Han Dynasty, known for his integrity and wisdom.
  • 羯鼓 - A type of drum used in ancient Chinese music, often associated with courtly entertainment.
  • 泰伯 - Taibo, one of the Three Descendants of the Duke of Zhou, is celebrated in Chinese history for his virtue and self-sacrifice.

Proper Nouns

  • 王猛 - A historical figure, a usurper who established the Xin Dynasty.
  • 陽貨 - A historical figure, a minister during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 豫讓 - A historical figure, a loyal assassin during the Warring States period.
  • 第五倫 - A historical figure, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Han Dynasty.
  • 泰伯 - A historical figure, one of the Three Descendants of the Duke of Zhou.

[217]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虽千万人吾往矣 - Famous quote from Mencius, meaning 'Even if there are a thousand people, I will go forward,' expressing unwavering determination and courage.
  • 牛山之水尝美矣 - Metaphorical expression from *Zuo Zhuan*, referring to the beauty of the water on Niu Mountain, used to suggest a moment of enlightenment or realization.
  • 大悟 - Means 'great enlightenment' or 'sudden realization.'
  • 言讫 - Means 'after speaking' or 'having spoken.'
  • 意颇自负 - Means 'felt quite proud' or 'was quite self-confident.'
  • 匿笑 - Means 'to laugh secretly' or 'to suppress laughter.'
  • 才人学博 - Refers to a talented person with extensive learning.
  • 惮食瓜征事 - Playful phrase that might be a pun or a reference to a specific anecdote, but it is unclear without more context.
  • 渠道得病关索 - Another unclear phrase, possibly a pun or a reference to a specific story or character.
  • 拚命三郎 - Refers to a character from *Water Margin* (Outlaws of the Marsh), known for his bravery and willingness to take risks.
  • 草元亭之扬子云 - Refers to Yang Xiong, a famous Han dynasty writer and scholar, known for his work *Fayan* (Words of Warning).
  • 颓阳货 - Derogatory term, possibly referring to someone who is lazy or idle.
  • 只晓得窃弓为盗 - Refers to the story of Yi Ya, a famous archer who stole a bow and was considered a thief.
  • 管甚子云子雨 - Phrase that might be a play on words, possibly referring to Yang Xiong's works or a pun on 'clouds and rain.'
  • 意窘 - Means 'feeling embarrassed' or 'feeling awkward.'
  • 姑饮三釂 - Means 'to drink three rounds' or 'to have three cups of wine.'
  • 书囊颇窄 - Means 'the book bag is quite narrow,' suggesting limited knowledge or resources.
  • 酒囊幸颇宽 - Means 'the wine bag is luckily quite wide,' suggesting that the person has a good capacity for drinking.
  • 酬酢移时 - Means 'the exchange of wine and food lasted for a while,' describing a social gathering.
  • 无诗 - Means 'no poem' or 'no verse.'
  • 集古人旧句 - Means 'to collect old phrases from ancient people.'
  • 一律 - Means 'one regulation' or 'one rule,' referring to a poetic form.
  • 夭斜儿 - Derogatory term, possibly referring to someone who is mischievous or unconventional.
  • 胸中亦有制度 - Means 'there is also a system in one's heart,' suggesting that the person has a certain level of discipline or order.
  • 先援笔而题曰 - Means 'first picked up the brush and wrote,' describing the act of writing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虽千万人吾往矣 - Famous quote from Mencius, emphasizing personal integrity and courage.
  • 牛山之水尝美矣 - Metaphor from *Zuo Zhuan*, often used to describe a moment of inspiration or realization.
  • 大悟 - Term often used in Buddhist and Confucian contexts to describe a sudden enlightenment.
  • 言讫 - Term used in classical Chinese to indicate the end of a speech or statement.
  • 意颇自负 - Term describing a state of pride or self-confidence.
  • 匿笑 - Term describing a suppressed or secret laugh.
  • 才人学博 - Term used to describe a talented and well-educated person.
  • 惮食瓜征事 - Playful phrase that might be a pun or a reference to a specific anecdote, but it is unclear without more context.
  • 渠道得病关索 - Another unclear phrase, possibly a pun or a reference to a specific story or character.
  • 拚命三郎 - Character from *Water Margin* (Outlaws of the Marsh), known for his bravery and willingness to take risks.
  • 草元亭之扬子云 - Refers to Yang Xiong, a famous Han dynasty writer and scholar, known for his work *Fayan* (Words of Warning).
  • 颓阳货 - Derogatory term, possibly referring to someone who is lazy or idle.
  • 只晓得窃弓为盗 - Refers to the story of Yi Ya, a famous archer who stole a bow and was considered a thief.
  • 管甚子云子雨 - Phrase that might be a play on words, possibly referring to Yang Xiong's works or a pun on 'clouds and rain.'
  • 意窘 - Term describing a state of embarrassment or awkwardness.
  • 姑饮三釂 - Term describing the act of drinking three rounds of wine.
  • 书囊颇窄 - Term describing a limited knowledge or resources.
  • 酒囊幸颇宽 - Term describing a good capacity for drinking.
  • 酬酢移时 - Term describing a social gathering that lasted for a while.
  • 无诗 - Term describing the absence of poetry or verse.
  • 集古人旧句 - Term describing the act of collecting old phrases from ancient people.
  • 一律 - Term describing a poetic form or regulation.
  • 夭斜儿 - Derogatory term, possibly referring to someone who is mischievous or unconventional.
  • 胸中亦有制度 - Term describing a state of having a system or order in one's heart.
  • 先援笔而题曰 - Term describing the act of writing or composing.

Proper Nouns

  • 大姨 - Refers to the eldest aunt.
  • 七姨 - Refers to the seventh aunt.
  • 三姨 - Refers to the third aunt.
  • 五姨 - Refers to the fifth aunt.
  • 某 - Refers to the speaker.
  • 《水浒》 - Refers to *Water Margin* (Outlaws of the Marsh), a famous Chinese novel.
  • 草元亭之扬子云 - Refers to Yang Xiong, a famous Han dynasty writer and scholar.
  • 颓阳货 - Derogatory term, possibly referring to someone who is lazy or idle.
  • 子云子雨 - Phrase that might be a play on words, possibly referring to Yang Xiong's works or a pun on 'clouds and rain.'

[218]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嫁得蕭郎愛遠游 - Literal meaning: 'married to Xiao Lang who loves traveling far'; introduces the theme of a woman whose husband is often away.
  • 每因風景卻生愁 - Literal meaning: 'often because of the scenery, she feels sorrow'; indicates that the natural beauty triggers her melancholy.
  • 桃花臉薄難藏淚 - Describes a woman with a delicate peach-blossom-like face that cannot hide her tears, suggesting vulnerability.
  • 桐樹心孤易感秋 - Refers to a solitary willow tree, symbolizing loneliness and sensitivity to autumn, which is often associated with melancholy in Chinese poetry.
  • 阆苑有書多附鶴 - "Làngyuàn" refers to a mythical garden of immortals, and "附鶴" means "attached to cranes," implying that messages or books are carried by cranes, a common motif in Chinese mythology.
  • 畫屏無睡待牽牛 - "畫屏" refers to a painted screen, and "牽牛" is the Chinese name for the constellation Altair, part of the legend of the Cowherd and Weaver Girl, suggesting a lonely night of waiting.
  • 旁人未必知心事 - Means 'others may not know her inner thoughts,' highlighting the woman's private emotional turmoil.
  • 又抱輕衾上玉樓 - "玉樓" refers to a luxurious or celestial building, and "輕衾" means "light quilt," suggesting the woman's solitude and the coldness of her surroundings.
  • 把酒堂前日又昏 - Means 'holding wine at the hall, the day again turns dark,' indicating a sense of time passing and the fading of joy.
  • 料得也應憐宋玉 - "宋玉" was a famous poet from the Warring States period, and this line suggests that the woman feels sympathy for the poet's unrequited love.
  • 肯教容易見文君 - "文君" refers to Zuo Zhen, a famous courtesan, and this line suggests that the woman is hesitant to easily reveal her true feelings.
  • 拋殘翠羽乘鸞扇 - "翠羽" refers to green feathers, and "鸞扇" means "phoenix fan," suggesting the loss of beauty and grace.
  • 惆悵金泥簇蝶裙 - "金泥" refers to golden mud, and "蝶裙" means "butterfly skirt," indicating a beautiful but now discarded garment.
  • 取次花叢懶回顧 - Means 'picking flowers carelessly without looking back,' suggesting a lack of interest in the beauty around her.
  • 淡紅香白一群群 - Describes a group of pale red and fragrant white flowers, indicating a contrast between the beauty of nature and the woman's indifference.
  • 與我周旋寧作我 - Means 'to interact with me, let it be me,' suggesting a desire for companionship and understanding.
  • 為郎憔悴卻羞郎 - Means 'for the sake of my husband, I wither but am ashamed of him,' indicating the woman's self-sacrifice and the complexity of her feelings.
  • 閒窺夜月銷金帳 - "銷金帳" refers to a luxurious tent, and this line suggests a moment of quiet reflection.
  • 倦倚春風白玉床 - "白玉床" means "white jade bed," indicating a luxurious and comfortable setting.
  • 誰為含愁獨不見 - Means 'who is the one who bears sorrow alone,' highlighting the woman's isolation.
  • 一生贏得是淒涼 - Means 'all she has won in life is sorrow,' summarizing the woman's fate.
  • 風景依稀似昔年 - Means 'the scenery vaguely resembles the past year,' suggesting a sense of nostalgia.
  • 畫堂金屋見婵娟 - "畫堂金屋" refers to a luxurious and beautiful hall, and "婵娟" means "graceful and beautiful," indicating a moment of beauty.
  • 曾經滄海難為水 - A famous line from a poem by Yuan Zhen, meaning 'once having seen the sea, other waters are not worth mentioning,' suggesting that the woman's experiences have made her less interested in lesser things.
  • 願作鴛鴦不羨仙 - Means 'I wish to be a mandarin duck and not envy immortals,' expressing a desire for simple, earthly happiness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蕭郎 - A generic term for a man, often used in poetry to refer to a beloved or husband.
  • 桃花臉 - A metaphor for a beautiful, delicate face, often associated with women.
  • 桐樹 - The willow tree, often used in Chinese poetry to symbolize loneliness and sadness.
  • 阆苑 - A mythical garden of immortals, often used in poetry to represent a place of beauty and transcendence.
  • 畫屏 - A painted screen, often used in poetry to represent a luxurious or idealized setting.
  • 牽牛 - The Chinese name for the constellation Altair, part of the legend of the Cowherd and Weaver Girl.
  • 宋玉 - A famous poet from the Warring States period, known for his works on unrequited love.
  • 文君 - Zuo Zhen, a famous courtesan, often used in poetry to represent a woman of beauty and talent.
  • 鸞扇 - A phoenix fan, often used in poetry to represent beauty and grace.
  • 蝶裙 - A butterfly skirt, often used in poetry to represent beauty and elegance.
  • 銀漢 - The Milky Way, often used in poetry to represent a vast and distant space.
  • 雲雨巫山 - The mythological encounter between the goddess Xi Wangmu and the mortal Yang Xi, often used in poetry to represent a moment of intense passion.
  • 鴛鴦 - Mandarin ducks, often used in poetry to represent a pair of lovers.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 嫁得蕭郎愛遠游，每因風景卻生愁 - The sentence structure is clear, but the emotional depth of the woman's feelings is conveyed through the juxtaposition of the husband's love for travel and her resulting sorrow.
  • 桃花臉薄難藏淚，桐樹心孤易感秋 - The parallel structure of the two lines emphasizes the woman's vulnerability and the natural triggers of her sadness.
  • 阆苑有書多附鶴，畫屏無睡待牽牛 - The parallel structure of the two lines creates a contrast between the mythical and the mundane, highlighting the woman's loneliness.
  • 旁人未必知心事，又抱輕衾上玉樓 - The sentence structure is straightforward, but the emotional weight of the woman's private thoughts and her physical solitude is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 夢來何處為更雲，把酒堂前日又昏 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of time passing and the fading of joy is conveyed through the imagery of the dream and the wine.
  • 料得也應憐宋玉，肯教容易見文君 - The sentence structure is clear, but the woman's feelings of sympathy and hesitation are conveyed through the references to the poets and courtesans.
  • 拋殘翠羽乘鸞扇，惆悵金泥簇蝶裙 - The parallel structure of the two lines emphasizes the loss of beauty and grace.
  • 取次花叢懶回顧，淡紅香白一群群 - The sentence structure is clear, but the contrast between the beauty of nature and the woman's indifference is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 本来銀漢是紅牆，雲雨巫山枉斷腸 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of separation and futility is conveyed through the references to the Milky Way and the mythological encounter.
  • 與我周旋寧作我，為郎憔悴卻羞郎 - The sentence structure is clear, but the complexity of the woman's feelings is conveyed through the juxtaposition of her desire for companionship and her self-sacrifice.
  • 閒窺夜月銷金帳，倦倚春風白玉床 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of quiet reflection and luxury is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 誰為含愁獨不見，一生贏得是淒涼 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of isolation and sorrow is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 風景依稀似昔年 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of nostalgia is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 畫堂金屋見婵娟 - The sentence structure is clear, but the moment of beauty is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 曾經滄海難為水 - The sentence structure is clear, but the sense of experience and disillusionment is conveyed through the imagery.
  • 願作鴛鴦不羨仙 - The sentence structure is clear, but the desire for simple, earthly happiness is conveyed through the imagery.

[219]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 归去岂知还向月 - Poetic expression suggesting a return to the moon, symbolizing a departure from the mundane world. '岂知' adds a layer of uncertainty or inevitability.
  • 坐来虽近远于天 - Paradoxical statement indicating that even though one is seated, they are far from heaven, possibly alluding to spiritual or existential distance.
  • 何时诏此金钱会 - Rhetorical question about when such a gathering of wealth or power will be convened, possibly alluding to a metaphorical or literal assembly.
  • 一度思量一惘然 - Phrase indicating that each time one reflects, they feel a sense of aimlessness or despair.
  • 黄鹤题诗，女青莲亦当束手 - Reference to the legendary Yellow Crane Tower and the poet Li Bai (青莲居士), suggesting that even a great poet like Li Bai would be stumped by this task.
  • 金屋装成贮阿娇 - Reference to the story of Emperor Wu of Han building a golden house for his concubine A-Jiao, symbolizing luxury and indulgence.
  • 酒香红被夜迢迢 - Poetic description of a luxurious and prolonged night, with the scent of wine and the warmth of a red quilt.
  • 瀛台月暗乘双凤 - Reference to the mythical Yingtai (Imperial Palace) where two phoenixes fly in the dark moonlight, symbolizing beauty and mystery.
  • 铜雀春深锁二乔 - Reference to the Copper Bird Terrace where the two Qiao sisters were confined, symbolizing captivity and loss.
  • 自有风流堪证果 - Phrase suggesting that there is a certain charm or allure that can be a proof of something, possibly spiritual or moral.
  • 更无消息到今朝 - Statement indicating that there has been no news or communication up to the present day.
  • 不如逐伴归山去 - Suggestion to follow companions back to the mountains, symbolizing a retreat from the world.
  • 渌水斜通宛转桥 - Poetic description of a clear, winding stream leading to a curved bridge, symbolizing a peaceful and natural setting.
  • 闲倚屏风笑周昉 - Reference to the painter Zhou Fang, suggesting a leisurely and artistic demeanor.
  • 不令仙犬吠刘晨 - Reference to the myth of Liu Shen and Yin Xi, where a dog barks at them upon their return from the fairyland, symbolizing a return to reality.
  • 相思相见如何日 - Rhetorical question about when one will see the one they long for, expressing longing and uncertainty.
  • 倾国倾城不在人 - Phrase suggesting that the beauty or power of a nation or city is not dependent on any one person, possibly alluding to the concept of collective strength.
  • 回首可恃歌舞地 - Phrase suggesting that one can rely on the place of music and dance, possibly alluding to a past life or a place of refuge.
  • 行尘不是昔时尘 - Statement indicating that the dust of travel is not the same as it was in the past, symbolizing change and the passage of time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黄鹤题诗 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Crane Tower, a famous landmark in Wuhan, where many poets have inscribed their works.
  • 女青莲 - Refers to Li Bai, a renowned Tang Dynasty poet known as the 'Immortal Poet' (青莲居士).
  • 金屋装成贮阿娇 - Refers to the story of Emperor Wu of Han building a golden house for his concubine A-Jiao, a symbol of luxury and indulgence.
  • 瀛台 - Refers to the mythical Yingtai (Imperial Palace), a place of beauty and mystery in Chinese mythology.
  • 铜雀春深锁二乔 - Refers to the Copper Bird Terrace where the two Qiao sisters were confined, a symbol of captivity and loss.
  • 周昉 - Refers to Zhou Fang, a Tang Dynasty painter known for his depictions of court ladies.
  • 仙犬吠刘晨 - Refers to the myth of Liu Shen and Yin Xi, where a dog barks at them upon their return from the fairyland, symbolizing a return to reality.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄鹤 - Refers to the Yellow Crane Tower, a famous landmark in Wuhan.
  • 青莲 - Refers to Li Bai, a renowned Tang Dynasty poet.
  • 阿娇 - Refers to the concubine A-Jiao of Emperor Wu of Han.
  • 瀛台 - Refers to the mythical Yingtai (Imperial Palace).
  • 铜雀 - Refers to the Copper Bird Terrace.
  • 二乔 - Refers to the two Qiao sisters.
  • 周昉 - Refers to Zhou Fang, a Tang Dynasty painter.
  • 刘晨 - Refers to Liu Shen, a figure in the myth of Liu Shen and Yin Xi.

[220]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大姨曰 - Indicates that the eldest sister is speaking.
  • 妮子出口便谈风月 - Colloquial expression meaning 'the young girl speaks of romantic and poetic matters as soon as she opens her mouth.'
  • 真个颠狂欲死 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'truly, she is madly passionate to the point of death.'
  • 谁似阿姊道学 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who is like my sister in her scholarly demeanor?'
  • 抱得轻衾上玉楼 - Poetic expression meaning 'to hold a light quilt on the jade tower,' symbolizing a romantic or poetic ideal.
  • 绮语撩人 - Phrase meaning 'flattering words that tempt or charm.'
  • 亦是女儿家本相 - Phrase meaning 'it is also the nature of a woman.'
  • 对酒当歌 - Phrase meaning 'to drink wine and sing,' often used to express a carefree and joyful attitude.
  • 楚囚之泣 - Phrase meaning 'the weeping of a Chu captive,' symbolizing deep sorrow or lamentation.
  • 风流蕴藉 - Phrase meaning 'graceful and refined.'
  • 洵是可人 - Phrase meaning 'truly delightful.'
  • 妹不能诗 - Phrase meaning 'I cannot compose poetry.'
  • 倩九姊捉刀可乎 - Phrase meaning 'can I ask my ninth sister to write for me?'
  • 平生原不解相思 - Phrase meaning 'I have never understood longing.'
  • 莫遣玲珑唱我词 - Phrase meaning 'do not let the clever one sing my words.'
  • 有酒惟浇赵州土 - Phrase meaning 'if there is wine, pour it only on the soil of Zhaozhou,' symbolizing a sense of disillusionment.

  • 目耽耽视焦 - "目耽耽" means 'eyes fixedly,' and "视焦" means 'looking at Jiao,' indicating that the cat fixed its eyes on the person.
  • 焦愈怒，张臂作擒缚状 - "焦愈怒" means 'Jiao became even angrier,' and "张臂作擒缚状" means 'spread his arms as if to catch and bind,' indicating that the person became even angrier and spread his arms as if to catch and bind the cat.
  • 而猫嗥然一声，曳尾徐步，过邻墙而去 - "嗥然一声" means 'let out a loud cry,' "曳尾" means 'dragging its tail,' "徐步" means 'slowly walked,' and "过邻墙而去" means 'went over the wall of the neighbor's house,' indicating that the cat let out a loud cry, dragged its tail, slowly walked, and went over the wall of the neighbor's house.

  • 间亦有改适者 - Occasionally there were women who remarried.
  • 百余年来，闺门清白，从无中冓之事 - For over a hundred years, the family maintained a reputation for purity and propriety, with no scandalous or improper behavior.
  • 文君私奔司马 - The story of Sima Xiangru and Zhuo Wenjun, where Zhuo Wenjun eloped with Sima Xiangru, which was considered scandalous.
  • 卓王孙勒令守寡 - Zhuo Wangsun (Zhuo Wenjun's father) forced her to remain a widow.
  • 得此可补闺箴之阙 - This story can fill a gap in the teachings about women's conduct.
  • 宋儒最讲礼法，何当时无一人议其后者 - The Song Confucians were most strict about ritual and law, so why did no one criticize Fan Zhongyan at the time?
  • 不能于昭昭伸节，犹愈于冥冥堕行也 - It is better to maintain one's virtue openly than to fall into improper behavior secretly.
  • 董相车边，宋王白畔 - Historical stories where officials or nobles were punished for improper behavior.
  • 益叹为千秋之仅事矣 - These stories are even more remarkable as rare events in history.

  • 桂桨兰桡 - 桂桨 refers to oars made of cassia wood; 兰桡 refers to paddles made of orchid wood. These terms are used metaphorically to describe elegant and luxurious boats.
  • 鱼龙百戏 - 鱼龙 refers to fish and dragons, often used in acrobatic performances; 百戏 means 'hundred plays' or a variety of entertainments. This phrase describes a spectacular performance involving acrobatics and other forms of entertainment.
  • 瞥见河畔柳渐作黄色 - 瞥见 means 'glimpsed'; 河畔柳 refers to willows by the river; 渐作黄色 means 'gradually turning yellow.' This phrase describes the fleeting sight of willows turning yellow, indicating the approach of autumn.
  • 珠帘高卷 - 珠帘 refers to a curtain made of pearls; 高卷 means 'raised high.' This phrase describes the luxurious and ornate setting of the scene.

  • 停鞭笑指，联辔徐行 - 停鞭 means 'stopping the whip'; 笑指 means 'pointing with a smile'; 联辔徐行 means 'slowly riding together.' This phrase describes a leisurely and joyful ride.
  • 满城风雨近重阳 - 满城风雨 means 'the whole city is shrouded in wind and rain'; 近重阳 refers to the approach of the Double Ninth Festival. This phrase is a poetic way of describing the approaching autumn season.
  • 诞麟儿 - 诞 means 'gave birth to'; 麟儿 refers to a child with the auspicious mark of a unicorn, symbolizing a precious and virtuous child. This phrase indicates the birth of a son.

  • 厕石作阶 - 厕石 (toilet stones), 作 (make), 阶 (steps). Describes making steps from toilet stones.

  • 生解衣就浴 - 生 (the man), 解衣 (take off his clothes), 就浴 (go to bathe). Describes the man taking off his clothes and going to bathe.

  • 筳上无他物，馁鱼败肉，葱滦蒜菹而已 - '筳' is a type of bamboo tray used for serving food. '馁鱼' means 'stale fish,' '败肉' means 'spoiled meat,' and '葱滦蒜菹' refers to pickled garlic and onions. This phrase describes a simple, unappetizing meal.
  • 生自浴后，亦渐不觉其臭 - '生' refers to the person being described, likely the protagonist. '自浴后' means 'after bathing,' and '渐不觉其臭' means 'gradually no longer noticing the smell.'

  • 气佳哉！蕉莸可同器矣 - '气佳哉' means 'good breath!' '蕉莸可同器矣' is an idiom meaning 'bananas and weeds can be in the same container,' which implies that good and bad things can coexist or that the person's breath has improved to the point where even unpleasant things can be tolerated.
  • 孔翁闻其事，不信，访于客馆 - '孔翁' refers to a respected elder or scholar named Kong. '闻其事' means 'heard of the matter,' and '访于客馆' means 'visited the guesthouse.'

  • 气佳哉！蕉莸可同器矣 - The idiom 'bananas and weeds can be in the same container' is a classical expression that emphasizes the coexistence of good and bad things, reflecting a philosophical attitude toward life's imperfections.
  • 孔翁 - The character 'Kong' (孔) is a common surname in Chinese history, often associated with Confucian scholars or respected elders. The term '孔翁' (Kong翁) suggests a wise and respected figure.
  • 莫逆交 - The term '莫逆交' refers to a deep, unbreakable friendship, a concept that has been celebrated in Chinese literature and philosophy for centuries.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 廟祝 - Refers to a temple caretaker or priest, who would typically be responsible for maintaining the temple and conducting rituals.
  • 樗蒲 - An ancient form of dice game that was popular in China during the Han dynasty. It involved throwing dice and betting on the outcome.
  • 香火 - Refers to incense and offerings, which were essential in religious rituals and symbolized devotion and worship.
  • 籌馬 - Counting sticks and markers used in gambling and other games of chance. *籌* are wooden or bamboo sticks used for counting, while *馬* are markers or tokens representing different values.
  • 香車 - A luxurious carriage, often decorated with fragrant materials, symbolizing wealth and status.
  • 新婦 - Refers to a newly married wife, often used in the context of dowries and marital obligations.
  • 黑心賊 - A derogatory term used to insult someone who is untrustworthy or dishonest, often in the context of financial or moral betrayal.
  • 脫簪珥夤緣得一官 - A reference to the practice of removing hairpins (*簪珥*) as a sign of respect or submission, and using connections (*夤緣*) to gain an official position. This reflects the social and political dynamics of the time, where personal connections and favors were often necessary for advancement.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence lists the various tasks managed by the protagonist: 'The kitchen staff and the music department, along with all trivial matters, were managed by him.'
  • This sentence describes the protagonist's return to his humble origins in a dream: 'Exhausted, he went to bed, and in an instant, he found himself back in the old, broken bed and leather cot from his wet nurse's home.'
  • This sentence shows the protagonist's initial disbelief in the dream: 'Initially suspecting it was an unreal dream, he dismissed it with a laugh.'
  • This sentence describes the protagonist's return to the same place and the preparations for the wedding: 'The next night, he returned to the same place, and there were servants waiting at the door, saying, 'The bride's carriage has departed. Please, the new nobleman, change your clothes and wait.''
  • This sentence describes the grandeur of the wedding venue: 'At that moment, the red candles burned brightly, the flutes and songs played continuously, and the multi-tiered corridors and pavilions were everywhere decorated with brocade canopies.'
  • This sentence describes the wedding ceremony and the couple's entry into the inner chambers: 'Shortly after, the colorful sedan chair stopped, dozens of lanterns surrounded it, and flower-patterned carpets were laid out. After the exchange of bows, the couple was led into the inner chambers.'
  • This sentence describes the intimate setting of the couple's first night together: 'In the shadows of the candlelight, she peeked from behind her fan, even more dignified than at first sight.'
  • This sentence describes the couple's intimate moments and the joy of marriage: 'He slowly removed his clothes, and they embraced into the heavy curtains. The joy of husband and wife, surpassing the pleasure of painting eyebrows.'

  • 月府仙葩 - Refers to the flowers of the moon palace, symbolizing something divine or extraordinary.
  • 鹫岭 - Reference to the mountain where the Buddha is said to have attained enlightenment, symbolizing a place of spiritual purity.
  • 八百年前 - Refers to a period 800 years before the present, indicating a long time ago.
  • 月主新广寒殿 - Refers to the moon god building a new palace in the cold palace (a metaphor for the moon), suggesting a significant event in the moon's history.
  • 命吴质移去 - The moon god orders Wu Qi to move the tree, where Wu Qi is a figure from Chinese mythology.
  • 罡风吹堕尘世 - Refers to a strong wind (罡风) blowing the tree down to the earthly world, indicating a supernatural event.
  • 钱神拾取 - The god of wealth picks up the tree, suggesting that the tree has fallen into the hands of a powerful figure.
  • 植诸铜山之上 - The tree is planted on a mountain of copper, indicating a place of great value or importance.
  • 其香忽敛 - The fragrance of the tree suddenly diminishes, suggesting a loss of its former glory.
  • 铜臭逼人 - Refers to the unpleasant smell of copper, often used to describe something that is overly materialistic or greedy.
  • 簸弄狡狯伎俩 - Refers to cunning or deceitful tricks, suggesting that the tree's current state is due to some form of manipulation.
  • 矫情者之自取也 - Refers to someone who is being hypocritical or insincere, suggesting that the tree's current state is a result of its own actions.
  • 吾当为花一洗此辱 - The speaker says they will cleanse the flower of this disgrace, indicating a desire to restore its former glory.
  • 亡何，异香飘拂，馨闻数里 - After a short time, a strange fragrance spreads, and it can be smelled for several miles, indicating a dramatic change.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 与子同归 - Poetic expression meaning 'to return together with you,' often used in a farewell or parting context.
  • 西风又作 - Literally 'the west wind again blows,' used to describe the wind rising or changing direction.
  • 烟飞雾卷 - Vivid description of smoke and fog dispersing or swirling, often used to create a sense of mystery or transformation.
  • 美人忽不见 - Literally 'the beautiful person suddenly disappears,' a common trope in classical literature to signify the fleeting nature of beauty or the supernatural.
  • 得今夕一番游戏 - Means 'to have a game tonight,' referring to a playful or magical interaction.
  • 无声之声，乃为正声 - Paradoxical statement meaning 'the soundless sound is the true sound,' suggesting that true beauty or essence lies in subtlety or absence.
  • 无味之味，乃为至味 - Paradoxical statement meaning 'the tasteless taste is the ultimate taste,' emphasizing the idea of purity or simplicity.
  • 归真反璞 - Means 'to return to simplicity and purity,' a common theme in Chinese philosophy and literature.
  • 终身不辱 - Means 'to remain unshamed or uncorrupted throughout life,' suggesting moral integrity.
  • 淇园绿竹，尽塞瓠河 - Metaphorical expression referring to the lush bamboo groves of the Qi Garden being used to block the Hù River, symbolizing the misuse of natural resources.
  • 钟寺乔松，且充麈尾 - Refers to the tall pines of the Bell Temple being used as dust brushes, another metaphor for the exploitation of natural beauty.
  • 蔡中郎座上琴材，亦曾从爨下来也 - Historical allusion to Cai Yong, a famous musician, whose musical instruments were made from wood that once burned in the stove, symbolizing the transformation of humble materials into something valuable.
  • 有志之士，尚当以此为法 - Means 'men of ambition should take this as an example,' encouraging emulation of the qualities described.
  • 俗传鹊报吉，鸦报凶 - Common folk belief that magpies bring good news and crows bring bad news.
  • 闻鹊噪者，咸有喜色 - Means 'those who hear the magpie's call are all filled with joy,' reflecting the positive associations with magpies.
  • 一闻鸦声，群必厌逐之 - Means 'upon hearing the crow's sound, everyone will surely chase it away,' reflecting the negative associations with crows.
  • 庭中旧植槐树一株 - Means 'an old locust tree planted in the courtyard,' setting the scene for the narrator's observations.
  • 鸦巢共颠 - Means 'the crow's nest is on the top,' indicating the crow's elevated position in the tree.
  • 遇雨晨雪夕 - Means 'when it rains or snows in the morning and evening,' describing the weather conditions.
  • 鸦无所得食 - Means 'the crow has nothing to eat,' highlighting the crow's struggle for survival.
  • 必设米于庭而饲之 - Means 'he must set out rice in the courtyard to feed it,' showing the narrator's compassion.
  • 每当朝曦初上 - Means 'every time the morning light first rises,' setting the time of day for the crow's activities.
  • 刷项梳翎，翘尾侧目 - Describes the crow's grooming behavior, including cleaning its neck, combing its feathers, and lifting its tail and turning its eyes.
  • 备极其态 - Means 'displaying all its postures,' emphasizing the crow's graceful movements.
  • 独不善于鸣 - Means 'but it is not good at singing,' noting the crow's inability to produce a pleasing sound.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 必设米于庭而饲之 - Shows the narrator's compassion and care for the crow, which can be seen as a moral lesson.

  • 刷项梳翎，翘尾侧目 - These descriptions of the crow's grooming behavior can be seen as a metaphor for self-care and preparation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 即命妇抱儿入署 - Literally 'immediately ordered the wife to bring the child into the office'; '署' refers to the official residence or office.
  • 少选，儿系铃悬锁，花红绣葆而出 - '少选' means 'after a short while'; the description of the child being tied with a bell and locked with a flower-red embroidered lock is highly symbolic.
  • 蒙太夫人优赏，许螟蛉作孙儿矣 - '蒙太夫人优赏' means 'graciously received the favor of the elder lady'; '许螟蛉作孙儿矣' refers to adopting the child as a grandson, where '螟蛉' is a term for an adopted child.
  • 若儿即我儿，幸善视之 - '若儿即我儿' means 'if the child is my child'; '幸善视之' means 'please take good care of him'.
  • 他日功名，勿使出我下可耳 - '勿使出我下可耳' means 'do not let him surpass me in rank or achievement'.
  • 尔众中有明理之士，幸谅予心，勿以前言为河汉也 - '尔众中有明理之士' means 'among you there are those who understand reason'; '勿以前言为河汉也' means 'do not treat my previous words as empty talk'.
  • 两家之羞尽释 - '羞' means 'shame'; '尽释' means 'completely resolved'.
  • 后儿读书食饩于庠，奉公长生禄位，朝夕供养焉 - '庠' refers to a school; '奉公长生禄位' means 'uphold the official's longevity and position'.
  • 含垢纳污之说，为临民者言 - '含垢纳污' means 'to bear filth and dirt'; '为临民者言' means 'for those who govern the people'.
  • 此印板律例，非读书人不能解也 - '印板律例' refers to printed legal codes; '非读书人不能解也' means 'only those who have studied can understand it'.
  • 舍身以保赤子 - '舍身' means 'to sacrifice oneself'; '保赤子' means 'to protect the innocent'.
  • 类非守经者所能 - '守经' means 'to adhere to established rules'.
  • 现不坏身，运广长舌 - '不坏身' means 'an uncorrupted body'; '运广长舌' means 'to use a wide and long tongue', referring to eloquence.
  • 向讼庭为众生说法耶 - '讼庭' means 'court'; '为众生说法' means 'to preach to all beings'.
  • 黄盖以武人而治石城，况钟以小吏而治吴郡 - '黄盖' and '况钟' are historical figures; '治' means 'to govern'.
  • 冯坚、王兴宗辈 - These are historical figures.
  • 或以典史，或以直厅 - '典史' and '直厅' refer to official positions.
  • 王晋溪谓吏治之善，不必出于甲科 - '王晋溪' is a historical figure; '吏治之善' means 'good governance'; '不必出于甲科' means 'does not necessarily come from the highest ranks'.
  • 失油络者必受飞灾 - '失油络' means 'to lose the oil net'; '必受飞灾' means 'will certainly suffer a flying disaster'.
  • 授沐枕者终成冤狱 - '授沐枕' means 'to give a bath and pillow'; '终成冤狱' means 'will eventually become a wrongful case'.
  • 学优则仕，旨哉是言 - '学优则仕' means 'those who excel in learning will become officials'; '旨哉是言' means 'what profound words these are'.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 延烧庐舍 - Means 'to burn down houses,' where '延烧' refers to the spreading of fire.
  • 室中物靡有孑遗 - Means 'all things in the room were completely destroyed,' where '靡有孑遗' is a literary expression indicating total loss.
  • 后竟以贫死 - Means 'he eventually died in poverty,' where '竟' emphasizes the final outcome.
  • 读书不能致富，此言是矣 - Means 'the saying that reading cannot make one rich is true,' where '是矣' affirms the statement.
  • 然以求富之念读书，吾知其非读书人 - Means 'but if one reads with the desire for wealth, I know he is not a true reader,' where '然' introduces a contrast.
  • 我辈读圣贤书，所学何事 - Means 'We who read the books of sages, what do we learn?' where '我辈' refers to 'we, the readers.'
  • 乃以富贵利达，横亘于中 - Means 'but with the pursuit of wealth and status, it obstructs the mind,' where '横亘' means 'to block or obstruct.'
  • 稍不得志，辄归咎于书 - Means 'if one fails slightly, he immediately blames the books,' where '辄' means 'immediately' or 'always.'
  • 试请掩却书本，毕竟向何处觅生活哉 - Means 'try covering the book, where will you find a living?' where '毕竟' emphasizes the final outcome.
  • 甲子仲秋 - Refers to the middle of the eighth month in the year of Jiazi (a term in the Chinese zodiac).
  • 惟吾与书，盟于草堂 - Means 'only I and the books, made a pact in the thatched hut,' where '盟' means 'to make a pact.'
  • 言归于好 - Means 'to return to harmony,' a common phrase in classical Chinese.
  • 出则随车，归则并几 - Means 'when going out, it follows the carriage; when returning, it sits beside the table,' where '并几' means 'to sit beside the table.'
  • 夜火晨鸡总备尝 - Means 'enduring the night fires and morning roosters,' a metaphor for enduring hardships.
  • 吾怜汝，把牙签笑插，玳瑁亲装 - Means 'I pity you, laughing as you insert the bamboo slips, personally arranged in tortoiseshell,' where '牙签' refers to bamboo slips used for writing.
  • 谁知尔本无良，枉赚尽英雄而鬓苍 - Means 'who knew you are inherently untrustworthy, deceiving heroes and making them gray-haired in vain,' where '枉赚' means 'deceiving in vain.'
  • 叹臣饥欲死，千钟甚处 - Means 'sighing that I am starving to death, where is the thousand measures of grain?' where '千钟' is a metaphor for wealth.
  • 立锥无地，金屋何方 - Means 'having no place to stand even with a cone, where is the golden house?' where '立锥无地' means 'having no place to stand.'
  • 我自怜卿，卿真负我 - Means 'I pity you, but you truly betray me,' where '负' means 'to betray.'
  • 拔剑相看也不妨 - Means 'it is not wrong to draw the sword and look at each other,' a metaphor for confrontation.
  • 言未毕，书早惭而退，潜出门墙 - Means 'before the words were finished, the book felt ashamed and retreated, secretly leaving the door and wall,' where '潜' means 'secretly.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 學書不成，將焉學乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'If one fails to learn calligraphy, what else should one learn?' Reflects on the value of learning and the importance of choosing the right path.
  • 不如老農 - Literally means 'not as good as an old farmer,' used here to suggest that even a humble occupation like farming has its virtues.
  • 有草庐半亩，横塘之曲 - Describes a modest dwelling, 'a thatched cottage half an acre in size, nestled in the curve of a pond.'
  • 石田一顷，葑水之东 - Refers to 'stone fields one qing in size, east of the reed-covered water.'
  • 椎髫鸿妻，蓬头霸子 - Describes a simple, rustic lifestyle, 'with a wife who braids her hair into a bun and a son who lets his hair hang loose.'
  • 裹饭偕行荷锸从 - Means 'packing food and walking together with hoes in hand,' describing the simplicity and togetherness of rural life.
  • 桃源境，看桑麻鸡犬，乐也融融 - Refers to the idyllic setting of 'a Peach Blossom Spring, where one can see mulberry and hemp fields and hear the sounds of chickens and dogs, enjoying a harmonious and peaceful life.'
  • 悲哉吾道终穷，似稼圃樊迟术未工 - Expresses sorrow over the failure of one's path, comparing it to the unsuccessful attempt of Fan Chi to learn farming.
  • 枉操豚以祝，学齐东语 - Means 'in vain offering a pig for sacrifice and learning the dialect of Qi,' suggesting futile efforts.
  • 揠苗而槁，与宋人同 - Refers to the fable of the man who pulled up his seedlings to make them grow faster, resulting in their withering, symbolizing the folly of trying to force success.
  • 门有催科，瓶无储粟，庑下投人作赁春 - Describes the hardships of life, 'with tax collectors at the door, no grain stored in the jar, and seeking employment under the eaves in spring.'
  • 翻然悔，悔从来耕也，馁在其中 - Means 'suddenly regretting, regretting that one has always been a farmer, with hunger in it,' expressing a sense of resignation and self-reflection.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 学书 - Refers to the practice of calligraphy, a highly valued art form in Chinese culture.
  • 老农 - Represents the humble but respected life of a farmer, often seen as a symbol of simplicity and virtue.
  • 桃源境 - Alludes to the 'Peach Blossom Spring' from the story of Tao Yuanming, a metaphor for an idealized, peaceful, and self-sufficient rural life.
  • 稼圃樊迟术未工 - Refers to Fan Chi, a disciple of Confucius, who tried to learn farming but was unsuccessful, as mentioned in the Analects.
  • 揠苗而槁 - A well-known fable from the 'Guanzi' text, illustrating the folly of trying to force growth.
  • 门有催科 - Refers to the practice of tax collection, a common source of hardship for peasants in ancient China.
  • 瓶无储粟 - Describes the scarcity of food, a common theme in depictions of poverty and hardship.
  • 庑下投人作赁春 - Refers to the practice of seeking temporary employment under the eaves of a house during the spring season, a common form of labor for the poor.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The rhetorical question '学书不成，将焉学乎？' and the subsequent comparison to a farmer create a contrast between intellectual pursuits and practical, humble work.
  • The parallel structure '有草庐半亩，横塘之曲；石田一顷，葑水之东' describes the modest and idyllic setting of the speaker's life.
  • The long sequence '椎髫鸿妻，蓬头霸子，裹饭偕行荷锸从，桃源境，看桑麻鸡犬，乐也融融' describes the simple, peaceful life of the speaker, with a focus on the harmony between humans and nature.
  • The comparison to Fan Chi's failed attempt at farming in '悲哉吾道终穷，似稼圃樊迟术未工' adds a layer of self-reflection and sorrow to the speaker's realization of their own limitations.
  • The two fables '枉操豚以祝，学齐东语；揠苗而槁，与宋人同' serve as metaphors for the speaker's futile efforts and the natural consequences of trying to force success.
  • The sequence '门有催科，瓶无储粟，庑下投人作赁春' describes the hardships of life, with a focus on the struggles of the poor.
  • The final line '翻然悔，悔从来耕也，馁在其中' expresses a sense of resignation and self-reflection, acknowledging the inevitability of hunger and hardship.

  • 正藐姑仙子，導予翠節 - Miǎo Gū Xiānzǐ is a mythical figure associated with immortality and the supernatural. Cui Jié refers to a jade staff, symbolizing authority or guidance.
  • 金門谪吏，坐我霜虬 - Jīn Mén refers to the Golden Gate in the Penglai fairyland, a symbol of immortality. Zé lì means 'exiled official,' and Shuāng qiú refers to a frosty dragon, symbolizing power or authority.
  • 笑酌流霞，醉眠芳草 - Liú Xiá refers to a mythical drink or wine that flows like clouds, symbolizing immortality or transcendence. Fāng Cǎo means 'fragrant grass,' often used metaphorically to represent a peaceful or idyllic place.
  • 眼看蓬莱弱水流 - Pénglái is a mythical island of immortality, and Ruò Shuǐ refers to a mythical river that is difficult to cross, symbolizing the separation between the mortal and immortal realms.
  • 丹炉妙诀，压倒浮邱 - Dān Lú refers to an alchemical furnace used in the pursuit of immortality. Miào Jué means 'wonderful secret' or 'mysterious technique.' Fú Qiū is a mythical figure often associated with immortality.
  • 茂陵风雨堪愁，伴寂寞骊山碧树秋 - Mào Líng and Lí Shān are historical sites associated with the Han and Tang dynasties, respectively. The phrase suggests a sense of melancholy and solitude.
  • 叹莫须有者，壶公桂父 - Mò Xū Yǒu means 'without cause' or 'groundless,' and Hú Gōng and Guì Fù are mythical figures often associated with immortality.

  • 書汝來前，與子別後，益復無聊 - Formal address suggesting that the speaker has become more lonely or bored since parting from someone.
  • 倘蒙君見宥，仍開舊閣 - Jiàn Yòu means 'to forgive,' and the phrase suggests that the speaker hopes to be forgiven and have access to a former place or privilege.
  • 謂予不信，再訂新交 - Rhetorical question suggesting that the speaker doubts the other person's trustworthiness and is considering making a new alliance.
  • 苟蹈前愆，有如皦日 - Qián Qiān means 'past mistakes,' and Jiǎo Rì refers to the bright sun, symbolizing clarity or truth.
  • 從此相攜卧草茅 - Cǎo Máo refers to humble or rustic dwellings, symbolizing a simple or secluded life.

  • 视为谈天炙輠则过矣 - Phrase meaning 'to regard it as mere idle chatter or a worthless object would be an overstatement,' implying that the subject is more significant than simple talk or a trivial thing.
  • 薄俗无青眼 - Phrase meaning 'the shallow world has no favor for me,' expressing the idea that the speaker is not valued or respected in society.
  • 高堂有白头 - Phrase meaning 'my parents have grown old,' conveying the speaker's concern for their aging parents.
  • 临行重怅望 - Phrase meaning 'on the eve of departure, I feel heavy with longing,' describing the emotional state of someone about to leave, filled with regret and nostalgia.
  • 空作稻粱谋 - Phrase meaning 'in vain, I plan for sustenance,' suggesting that the speaker's efforts to secure a living are futile or unfulfilled.

  • 左曰：文字案鬼犯四名 - Phrase indicating that on the left side, there are four ghosts accused of literary offenses.

  • 独我辈流堕地狱 - Phrase indicating that unlike the aforementioned figures, these writers have fallen into hell.

  • 独我辈流堕地狱 - Phrase indicating that unlike the aforementioned figures, these writers have fallen into hell, suggesting a contrast between their fate and that of other famous writers.
